text: The surgeon places a VisiPort in the left upper quadrant and dissects adhesions between the omentum and abdominal wall, revealing multiple loops of small bowel adhered to the abdominal wall, particularly in the right lower quadrant. A 12mm port is inserted superumbilically through a small incisional hernia defect, with visible anastomotic staples indicating the ilio-ileo anastomosis.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection to carefully separate adhesions between the small bowel loops and the abdominal wall, avoiding energy devices near the bowel. The Studer pouch is identified by injecting 400 milliliters of saline diluted with methylene blue through a Foley catheter, with the blue dye visibly outlining the pouch wall.

The surgical view shows the ileocolic junction and the ileo-ileo anastomosis approximately twelve centimeters from the ileocecal valve. A right mid-abdominal five millimeter port is placed to assist with dissection and retraction. The surgeon dissects posterior to the ileo-ileo anastomosis, separating loops of small bowel and exposing the posterior wall of the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection between the loops of small bowel and the antireflux nipple valve. Due to the presence of adhesions obscuring the iliocolic pedicle, the surgical team opts for lateral to medial mobilization instead of the usual medial to lateral approach for the right colectomy.

The surgeon mobilizes the colon along the white line, separating the omentum from the transverse colon and continuing dissection until reaching the duodenum. The right ureter is visualized as the dissection progresses medially towards the antireflux nipple valve.

The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery to isolate the ileocolic pedicle, ensuring it is distant from the duodenum. The vasculature, which supplies the terminal ileum and colon, is then transected using a harmonic energy device.

The surgeon assesses the perfusion of the Studer pouch using indocyanine green injection, confirming intact perfusion of the pouch and nipple valve. The ileocolic pedicle has been taken down, and the remainder of the ileal and colonic mesentery is divided using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon externalizes the specimen, including the ilioilial anastomosis and six centimeters of proximal ileum. An extracorporeal anastomosis is constructed, and indocyanine green is injected to confirm perfusion. The specimen is returned inside, hemostasis is confirmed, and the pedicle is hemostatic. The duodenum remains intact, and the studer pouch and antireflux nipple valve are confirmed to be well vascularized.
